reasoning: The 1-year Treasury bill rate has fallen from 3.44% in early December to 3.34% by January 2, 2026, driven by market expectations of rate cuts in 2026 despite the Fed signaling just one cut. Due to this downward momentum in the very short term, the linear projection for January 11 favors a lower rate, leading to an estimate around 38%. Over a slightly longer horizon to February 3, the FOMC meeting in late January will be key. Since the market is only pricing in a 25.5% chance of a cut in January 2026, steady rates could cause a slight upward correction in yields, making an increase more plausible, moving the probability to 44%. By April 4, uncertainty dominates; if the strong US economy forces the market to price out additional cuts and align with the Fed's hawkish dot plot, yields will rise. Consequently, the probability reverts closer to the 50% base rate, settling at 49%.
